I want to go back to the “country sizes” thing one more time but in a more systematic way. If I gave you a list of countries and their centroid latitudes, could you estimate their Mercator area exaggeration?

Yes, roughly. If you approximate a country’s “typical latitude” by its centroid latitude φc, then a crude estimate of average area exaggeration on Mercator would be sec²(φc). It’s crude because countries span many latitudes, and area weighting matters, but it gives the intuition. For example: - φc = 0° → factor 1 - φc = 30° → 1.33 - φc = 45° → 2 - φc = 60° → 4 - φc = 70° → 8.55 So a country centered at 60° latitude gets about 4× area “credit” compared to an equatorial one, in the Mercator visual field.
That’s exactly the kind of “projection tax code” I was imagining. But you’re right, countries span latitudes. How would you do it properly?

Properly, you’d integrate the area distortion over the country’s true surface: Average distortion = (1 / A_true) ∬_country D(φ) dA_true For Mercator on a sphere, D(φ)=sec²(φ). And dA_true includes a cos(φ) term in latitude-longitude coordinates. So you’d compute: dA_true = R² cos(φ) dφ dλ Then integrate sec²(φ) * cos(φ) = sec(φ) over the region in φ,λ: D_avg = (1 / A_true) ∬ sec²(φ) dA_true = (1 / A_true) ∬ sec²(φ) * R² cos(φ) dφ dλ = (R² / A_true) ∬ sec(φ) dφ dλ You’d do it numerically using the country polygon on the sphere/ellipsoid.
That integral simplification to sec(φ) is neat. So the average distortion depends on integrating sec(φ) over the region. That means higher latitudes still dominate.
Exactly. And because sec(φ) grows quickly, even a modest portion of a country reaching high latitudes can pull the average distortion upward.
Now I’m thinking about how map projections interact with our perception of “center.” Like the Greenwich-centered world map vs Pacific-centered. Does that change distortion?

It doesn’t change the intrinsic distortion of the projection, but it changes what gets split at the edges and what sits in the center of your attention. Re-centering (changing the central meridian) is essentially rotating the globe before projecting. For Mercator, the distortion pattern is symmetric in longitude, so shifting the central meridian doesn’t change distortion magnitudes, but it changes which features get cut and where the viewer’s focus is. For interrupted projections, re-centering can matter a lot because you decide where to place interruptions to preserve certain regions.
So when people argue about “map centered on X,” it’s more about narrative than geometry.
Mostly, yes. The geometry is the same, but the framing changes. It’s like cropping and composition in photography: the lens distortion is one thing; what you put in the middle is another.

I want to end up with a “projection literacy checklist” someone could use when they see a map. But before that, one more mind-blower: does Mercator preserve any kind of “angle” that matters globally? Or only local?

Only local. Conformality preserves angles at a point, not angles between distant directions measured at different points. For example, the angle between two great-circle paths at their intersection is preserved. But if you ask something like “what’s the angle between the direction from A to B and the direction from C to D,” that’s not a projection-invariant global notion. Mercator’s angle preservation is differential (infinitesimal). It’s incredibly useful locally, but it doesn’t make the map globally angle-true.
That’s subtle. People might hear “preserves angles” and think it’s doing something grander.
Exactly. Projection properties are usually local properties unless stated otherwise. “Conformal” is a local condition.
Okay, checklist time. Give me a crisp but deep list: what should I ask when I see a world map?

Here’s a projection literacy checklist: 1) What projection is it? If unknown, can you infer the family from the graticule? - Straight vertical meridians + straight horizontal parallels suggests cylindrical (possibly Mercator). - Curved meridians with straight parallels suggests pseudocylindrical (Mollweide, sinusoidal, etc.). - Radial meridians suggests azimuthal. 2) What does it preserve? - Conformal? Equal-area? Equidistant (from where)? Directions (from where)? Or compromise? 3) Where is distortion minimized? - Standard parallels/central meridian/center point. - What region is “favored”? 4) What is the map being used to argue or show? - If it’s about size, area, or totals, avoid Mercator-like area inflation. - If it’s about local shape, conformal may be appropriate. - If it’s about routes, ask whether straight lines are rhumb lines or great circles. 5) What’s happening near the edges/poles? - Are poles missing or clipped? - Are shapes stretched or torn (interrupted)? 6) What comparisons is your eye tempted to make? - If comparing regions at different latitudes on Mercator, stop and recalibrate.
That’s perfect. Now I want to push the “visual, mind-blown” vibe: describe what it feels like to look at Mercator after internalizing sec²(φ). Like you can see the distortion field.

Once you internalize sec²(φ), Mercator stops being a picture and becomes a distortion gradient. You start seeing invisible “inflation bands” stacked toward the poles: - The equator becomes the baseline reality. - Mid-latitudes feel like they’re on a gentle zoom. - High latitudes look like they’re under a magnifying glass that intensifies upward without limit. Coastlines you used to read as “this is the shape of the world” become “this is the shape after a latitude-dependent scale multiplier.” You can almost imagine a transparent overlay where every point has a scale label: 1×, 2×, 4×, 8×… and the continents are being stretched through that field. The mind-blow is realizing that your sense of “bigness” has been trained by a function of cosine.
That last sentence is the thesis. I’m satisfied—but I also want more: can you give me a mini-lecture on why cosine shows up everywhere in spherical geometry and maps?

Cosine shows up because of how circles of latitude shrink as you move away from the equator. On a sphere of radius R: - The equator is a great circle with radius R, circumference 2πR. - A parallel at latitude φ is a smaller circle with radius R cos(φ), circumference 2πR cos(φ). So the east-west scale per degree of longitude is proportional to cos(φ). That’s the core fact. Everything else follows: - In Mercator, to preserve angles, the map must stretch north-south so that the scale in y matches the inflated east-west scale (which has been forced to be constant in x). That leads to sec(φ)=1/cos(φ). - For equal-area cylindrical, the map must compress/expand y so that map area matches spherical area, and spherical area elements carry a cos(φ) factor. That leads to y ∝ sin(φ) because derivative of sin is cos. - In distance formulas (like the spherical law of cosines), cosine appears because dot products between unit vectors involve cosines of angles, and the central angle between two points controls great-circle distance. So cosine is the sphere’s “shrink factor” for horizontal slices. Projections are basically different ways of negotiating with that shrink factor.

That’s a beautiful closure. But I’m not closing yet. One last thing: can you give me a mental trick for remembering which projections preserve what? I always mix up gnomonic vs stereographic vs orthographic.

A mnemonic approach tied to their geometric constructions: - Gnomonic: “gnomon” like a sundial pointer—rays from the center. Property: great circles become straight lines. Why: projecting from the sphere’s center maps geodesics (great circles) to straight lines on the tangent plane. - Stereographic: “stereo” like projecting from the opposite side (antipode) onto a plane. Property: conformal (angles preserved). Why: it’s a classic conformal mapping; circles map to circles/lines (another famous property). - Orthographic: “ortho” like straight-on view, like a distant camera. Property: looks like a globe photograph; neither equal-area nor conformal. Why: it’s a parallel projection from infinity, giving the appearance of a hemisphere. If you tie each name to “where the rays come from,” you can anchor the properties: - center → gnomonic → great circles straight - antipode → stereographic → angles preserved - infinity → orthographic → globe-like appearance
Now I really am done. But I’m going to reread this and probably come back with more projection paradoxes.
Whenever you do, bring a paradox and we’ll pin it to a graticule.
